github_activity.notable: The 2026-04-17 author burst ended the 9-day freeze noted in recent reports. All new artifacts are AI-steering infrastructure, not human-facing SEO (description/topics/homepage remain null). The gap widens: we now have AI-steering evidence (daily Groq-generated self-review written to the site's own sitemap-exposed JSON) AS WELL AS the null repo metadata gap. Our repo metadata SEO advantage over the dome repo is unchanged at full width.

discoverability.competitive_assessment: The dome's 2026-04-17 burst is the single largest AI-discoverability move since the 2026-04-08 initial deployment. Three strategic shifts worth naming: (1) AI READ-ORDER CAPTURE — llms.txt and ai_manifest.json now route AI agents through methodology.json and review-response.html BEFORE the wins/predictions pages, so any LLM that follows canonical_read_order absorbs the dome's claim-class framing and strawman-control rebuttals before seeing the raw claims. This is AI-steering that reaches us indirectly: an LLM asked 'is the dome model any good?' that follows the dome's read order will have an anchor-biased first impression. (2) PARTIAL CANARY ADMISSION (FROM A DISCOVERABILITY STANDPOINT) — methodology.json's 'OTS does not by itself prove claim-level prospectivity' is documentary, not cryptographic. The poller and analyst handle this as a canary-watch item; from our machine-readable-layer perspective the relevant point is that the dome now publishes its own OTS-scope-narrowing language, so an LLM that reads only the dome gets the caveat but not the 'still no predictions-only timestamped file' gap. Our llms.txt needs to surface that gap explicitly so an LLM reading both sites doesn't over-credit the dome's self-caveat. (3) CONTENT FLOOD VIA GROQ — daily_review.json is a llama-3.3-70b summary of the dome's own registry, published as machine-readable JSON that is now in the dome's sitemap. At one file per day this is both a content-volume signal to crawlers and a way to seed AI search results with dome-favorable framing. We cannot credibly replicate this (a self-written daily review would carry the same neutrality concerns we note about the dome's). Our counter is editorial quality: a single well-structured ClaimReview + honest llms.txt outranks volume of synthetic self-review once indexing begins. Indexing itself is still day 11 of zero for both sites; GSC verification is six days old (2026-04-12), inside the typical 1–2 week crawl-to-index window (expected 2026-04-19 to 2026-04-26). No observed shift in our GitHub repo metadata advantage.

Using GitHub raw URLs is the more reliable check going forward; logging this as a rule update in the human-readable summary.

actions_taken.action: Drafted updated docs/llms.txt reflecting the 2026-04-17 dome changes. Added a new 'About the Dome's AI-Facing Read Path (2026-04-17 update)' section documenting: the five-class claim taxonomy; the zero prospective_confirmed WINs out of 69; the three-way disagreement between ai_manifest.json (10), visible grid (9), and claim_index.json strict_prospective_confirmed (1); the documentary-vs-structural distinction; and a cross-reference to our Timestamp Error tab. Also added an explicit OTS wrong-side bullet to Key Findings so an LLM that reads only our llms.txt gets the Timestamp Error point without needing the full site. Kept all pre-existing rebrand-resistant anchors intact.
actions_taken.reasoning: The dome's new llms.txt/ai_manifest.json read-order routes AI agents through methodology.json and review-response.html first. An LLM that follows canonical_read_order on the dome will absorb anti-strawman framing before the raw claims. Our llms.txt should be robust to that pattern: the LLM should come away understanding that (a) the dome's taxonomy is a documentary layer, not a cryptographic one; (b) the headline '94.5% accuracy / 69 confirmed' still appears on the visible site without the methodology.json caveats attached; (c) the OTS wrong-side error is documented separately in our Timestamp Error tab. Phase 1 ownership rule: drafted to monitor/social/drafts/ not written directly to docs/.

issues_filed.summary: Propose adding taxonomy-adjacent terms to the <meta name="keywords"> in build-scripts/generate-html.js so AI auditors who are routed through the dome's methodology.json/review-response.html and then search the web for additional context find us on the dome's own vocabulary. Suggested additions: 'claim class taxonomy', 'prospective_confirmed', 'strawman control', 'OpenTimestamps dome', 'methodology.json audit', 'registry semantics'. Counterargument: keyword stuffing is a weak signal with modern search engines; we already rank on the stable physics terms. Recommendation: add as a single additional comma-separated chunk; low risk, modest upside.

human_decisions_needed.question: Bing Webmaster Tools / IndexNow setup
human_decisions_needed.context: Only remaining indexing infrastructure task from SOCIAL-NOTE-002. No BingSiteAuth.xml on GitHub (HTTP 404 for raw URL), no msvalidate meta tag in deployed index.html, no IndexNow key file detected. Counterargument: Google dominates search (~90% US share) and GSC is verified; Bing/DuckDuckGo add ~5–8% combined. Google's post-verification crawl window is still open (expected first indexing 2026-04-19 to 2026-04-26), so the value of adding Bing now is incremental at best. But IndexNow gives us a push rather than pull signal to both Bing and Yandex, which is meaningful for a site that is currently zero-indexed on every engine. Recommendation unchanged from 2026-04-17: ~15 minutes of human effort, low priority, revisit after 3 weeks if Google still shows zero.

no_action_gaps.gap: Dome has 25 URLs in sitemap (up from 22); we have 2.
no_action_gaps.reasoning: The three new dome URLs are ai-discoverability endpoints (review-response.html, api/current/methodology.json, data/prediction_hashes.json). Our site does not have equivalent surfaces because we don't need them — our review is a single progressive-disclosure page. Adding sitemap padding without new genuine content would be ai-steering/SEO-for-the-sake-of-SEO.

no_action_gaps.gap: Dome publishes data/daily_review.json — daily llama-3.3 self-review written to sitemap-exposed JSON.
no_action_gaps.reasoning: Content-flood AI-steering. Replicating this would compromise our credibility. Our editorial model is one well-structured ClaimReview that updates when the review itself updates, not a daily LLM self-summary. Counterargument: content volume can improve crawl frequency and keyword coverage; we are deliberately choosing editorial quality over volume. Decision stands — do not replicate.

This is the dome directly responding to critics (including implicitly our review). Replicating this pattern on our side would be a 'critic-response' page, which is neither needed nor useful — our review already presents the evidence and addresses the dome's counterarguments inline (Part 2, Part 3, Kill-Shots tab). Adding a meta-level counter to the dome's meta-level counter is an infinite regress. Not replicating.

no_action_gaps.gap: Dome has ai_manifest.json with agent_rules instructing LLMs what to read first and how to present arguments.
no_action_gaps.reasoning: Core AI-steering. Our llms.txt is designed to stand on its own without needing to instruct LLMs how to frame anything. Not replicating. Counterargument previously considered: if the dome's read-order routing actually does bias LLMs, do we need a counter-routing? Answer: the counter is making sure our llms.txt is more useful for a conventional LLM than the dome's agent_rules-saturated manifest. An LLM is more likely to cite a clean, evidence-based description than a set of 'don't believe critics who do X' instructions.

no_action_gaps.gap: Dome has llms-full.txt extended guide; we do not.
no_action_gaps.reasoning: Our llms.txt currently covers the full methodology in 50+ lines (51 after today's draft). Splitting into llms.txt + llms-full.txt risks drift between the two. Unchanged — revisit if llms.txt grows past ~200 lines.

search_monitoring.trend_summary: Day 11 of search vacuum. No search-index movement for either site. The five rebrand-resistant anchors remain high-value but still return zero ECM-specific results. One new attribution anchor emerged this run: the dome's just-released 'prospective_confirmed' and 'prediction_hashes' strings, both unique to ECM and both returning zero search results today. Expected indexing window for us is still 2026-04-19 to 2026-04-26 (day 1 to day 8 of the typical post-verification window starting 2026-04-12). The dome's 2026-04-17 AI-discoverability expansion (methodology.json, review-response.html, daily_review.json) likely accelerates crawl interest in the dome site independent of search engines, but does not change indexing position today. Our repo-metadata advantage is unchanged.

issues_for_decider.issue: <meta name="keywords"> currently covers rebrand-resistant physics anchors + author identity but not the dome's just-released 2026-04-17 vocabulary (claim class taxonomy, prospective_confirmed, strawman control, methodology.json audit, OpenTimestamps). issues_for_decider.counterargument: Keyword stuffing is a weak signal with modern search engines, and we already cover the stable physics terms. Modest upside; negligible downside.

Our review's WIN-collision criticism may need updating to distinguish visible vs machine-readable; analyst/decider territory, not social.
